moveAnnotations.annotation: We open with e4, seizing the center and preparing for one of the most romantic openings in chess history. The Evans Gambit starts here -- with the King's Pawn controlling d5 and f5, opening lines for the bishop and queen. Every great attacking game from the 1800s to Kasparov's modern revivals begins with this move.
plans: Rapid development toward the Italian Game structure
Control the center and open lines for pieces
alternatives: d4 - Queen's Pawn, different character entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black responds symmetrically, occupying the center and opening the diagonal for the f8 bishop. This leads to the Open Game where both sides get active piece play early. Black's e5 pawn mirrors White's central claim and sets up the tactical battles that define 1.e4 e5 openings.
alternatives: c5 - the Sicilian, the most popular response at top levels

moveAnnotations.annotation: Knight to f3, attacking e5 and developing toward the kingside. This is the most natural second move -- we threaten the e5 pawn, develop a piece, prepare to castle, and control the d4 square. Black must respond to the threat, which keeps the initiative on our side. Every Italian Game and Evans Gambit flows through this move.
plans: Bc4 next for the Italian setup
Prepare rapid kingside castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends e5 with the knight, the most natural and principled response. Nc6 develops a piece to its best square while protecting the center. The knight on c6 also influences d4 and e5, making it a cornerstone of Black's position. We're now in the most common e4 e5 starting structure.
alternatives: Nf6 - the Petrov, very solid but less combative

moveAnnotations.annotation: Bishop to c4, entering the Italian Game. The bishop aims at f7, Black's Achilles' heel that's only defended by the king. From c4, the bishop controls the critical d5 square and supports future central play. We need this bishop on this diagonal for the Evans Gambit to work -- without the pressure on f7 and the center, the pawn sacrifice wouldn't make sense.
plans: Set up the Evans Gambit with b4
Maintain pressure on the a2-f7 diagonal
alternatives: Bb5 - the Ruy Lopez, equally strong but different philosophy

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop actively to c5, creating the Giuoco Piano position ('quiet game' -- though it's about to get very loud). The bishop on c5 mirrors White's pressure by eyeing f2 and controlling the a7-g1 diagonal. This is the position where Captain Evans, playing aboard a mail ship in 1827, invented the gambit we're about to play.
plans: Counter-pressure on f2 and the dark squares
Prepare d6 and solid development
alternatives: Nf6 - the Two Knights Defence, also sharp
Be7 - the Hungarian, more passive

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Evans Gambit! We sacrifice a whole pawn on b4 to lure Black's bishop away from the c5 square. Why? Because once that bishop moves, we're going to play c3 and d4, blasting open the center with a massive lead in development. This is one of the most principled gambits in chess -- we're not gambling, we're investing. A pawn for time, space, and a devastating initiative. Morphy, Kasparov, and Nakamura have all crushed opponents with this move.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White sacrifices the b-pawn to accelerate central play with c3 and d4.
plans: After Bxb4, play c3 to kick the bishop and prepare d4
Open the center while ahead in development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes the pawn -- declining the Evans Gambit with moves like Bb6 is possible but generally gives White a comfortable position for free. By accepting, Black gets a material advantage but falls behind in development. The bishop on b4 is awkwardly placed and will be kicked around, giving White exactly the tempi needed to build a monster center.
alternatives: Bb6 - the Evans Gambit Declined, solid but passive
Ba5 - keeps the bishop active but White still gets c3-d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Immediately kicking the bishop and preparing d4. This is the whole point of the Evans Gambit -- c3 attacks the bishop with tempo and sets up the d4 pawn push. After the bishop retreats, we'll play d4 with a huge center and rapid development. White's compensation for the pawn is already visible: we're about to have pawns on e4 and d4 while Black is scrambling to develop. The initiative is worth more than a pawn here.
plans: d4 next move to blow open the center
Develop all pieces rapidly while Black deals with the bishop
alternatives: O-O - castling first is a different gambit approach but c3 is sharper

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining the pin on the c3 pawn (it's pinned to the king along the a5-e1 diagonal). This is considered the main line and the best retreat. The bishop stays active on the a5-e1 diagonal and keeps some pressure on White's queenside. Compared to Be7 or Bc5, Ba5 is the most combative choice -- Black isn't giving up the initiative without a fight.
plans: Maintain the pin on c3 to slow White's d4 plans
Develop quickly and try to consolidate the extra pawn
alternatives: Be7 - safe retreat but passive, gives White easy d4
Bc5 - returns to the original square but blocks c7 pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: The center explodes! d4 is the culmination of the Evans Gambit -- we now have a massive pawn center on e4 and d4, attacking Black's e5 pawn. If Black takes on d4, we recapture with the bishop or open even more lines. The position is opening up and our pieces are going to pour into the center. This is what we sacrificed the pawn for -- look at all those open diagonals and the lead in development.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's e4-d4 center is dominant, attacking Black's e5 and opening lines for all pieces.
plans: Open the center and exploit the development advantage
Castle and bring the full army into the attack

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black takes on d4, which opens the center -- exactly what White wants in a gambit. But Black doesn't have a great alternative: if Black doesn't take, White plays dxe5 winning a pawn back with an even better position. So Black accepts that the center opens and tries to weather the storm. The challenge now is that White has open lines and rapid development, while Black still needs several moves to get organized.
alternatives: d6 - tries to hold the center but dxe5 dxe5, Qxd8+ is unpleasant

moveAnnotations.annotation: We castle into the attack! Rather than recapturing on d4 immediately, we get the king safe and bring the rook into play. The f1 rook will soon land on d1 or support the center. This is strong psychology too -- we're so confident in our position that we don't even bother taking the pawn back immediately. The threat of cxd4 is always there, and meanwhile we're fully developed while Black is still struggling.
plans: cxd4 when the time is right to establish a massive center
Develop with Ba3, Nbd2, or Qb3 to increase pressure
alternatives: cxd4 - recapturing immediately is also strong but O-O is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d6 to shore up the e5 square (which doesn't have a pawn anymore) and open a retreat for the bishop to b6. d6 is a solid, defensive move that tries to stabilize the position. Black's plan is simple: survive the initial onslaught, keep the extra pawn, and reach an endgame where material matters more than initiative. But White's lead in development means the middlegame comes first.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the center but creates a relatively passive structure.
plans: Stabilize and develop the remaining pieces
Keep the extra pawn and trade down to an endgame
alternatives: Nf6 - develops and attacks e4 but d6 is needed for the bishop retreat

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we recapture, establishing the ideal pawn center: e4 and d4. This is a textbook dream center -- two pawns abreast controlling c5, d5, e5, and f5. Combined with our castled king and developed pieces, this center gives us enormous spatial advantage. Every piece has open lines, the bishop on c4 is a monster, and the d4 pawn controls key squares. The Evans Gambit has delivered exactly what it promised.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The e4-d4 center is the pinnacle of the Evans Gambit -- total central domination for the cost of one pawn.
plans: Develop remaining pieces and build a kingside attack
Use the center pawns to restrict Black's pieces

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop retreats to b6, where it's solid but no longer putting pressure on the c3-e1 diagonal. From b6 the bishop eyes d4 and f2, which provides some counterplay, but it's mostly a defensive retreat. Black is trying to complete development and get the king castled. The bishop on b6 is decent but compared to White's actively placed pieces, it's clear who has the initiative.
plans: Pressure d4 and the f2 point
Support d5 break in the future
alternatives: Be7 - very passive but solid
Bc7 - similar idea to b6 but more cramped

moveAnnotations.annotation: Knight to c3, developing the last minor piece and adding more firepower to the center. The knight controls d5 -- a dream outpost -- and supports the e4 pawn. From c3 it can jump to d5 at the perfect moment for a devastating blow. We now have every piece developed or near-developed (just the c1 bishop and rooks to activate), while Black is still struggling to castle. This development lead is the entire compensation for the Evans Gambit pawn.
plans: Nd5 at the right moment for a crushing blow
Bg5 or Ba3 to complete development and increase pressure
alternatives: Bg5 - pins the knight immediately but Nc3 is more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black finally develops the kingside knight to f6, attacking e4 and preparing to castle. This is long overdue -- Black needed this knight in the game several moves ago. Nf6 is the most natural and necessary move, defending against White's central dominance and getting one step closer to castling. But White still has a significant development advantage and the powerful center. The Evans Gambit middlegame is just beginning, and White's initiative is very real.
plans: Castle kingside urgently
Challenge e4 with the Nf6 and look for d5 counterplay
alternatives: Na5 - harasses the Bc4 but leaves the kingside undeveloped

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the king's pawn, immediately claiming central space and opening diagonals for the bishop and queen. This move controls the crucial d5 and f5 squares, preventing Black from establishing pieces there. It's the most direct way to fight for the center and begins White's aggressive intentions in the Evans Gambit system.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a classical pawn center presence, with the e4 pawn controlling key central squares and supporting future piece development.
plans: Develop pieces quickly toward the center
Prepare Nf3 to support the center and attack e5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's central advance, claiming equal space in the center and opening lines for piece development. This pawn controls d6 and f6, preventing White's pieces from occupying these key squares. The symmetrical response leads to open, tactical positions where both sides will fight for piece activity.

White develops the knight to its most natural square, attacking the e5 pawn and preparing to castle kingside. This move supports White's central control while developing toward the center. The knight from f3 can later jump to powerful outposts like d4 or g5, and it clears the path for castling.
plans: Attack the e5 pawn to gain central pressure
Prepare kingside castling for safety

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with natural development, bringing the knight toward the center where it controls important squares. This knight supports Black's central pawn and prepares for further piece coordination. From c6, the knight eyes the d4 and b4 squares, potentially disrupting White's plans.

White develops the bishop to an aggressive square, targeting the f7 pawn - Black's weakest point in the opening. This move is the hallmark of the Italian Game family, putting immediate pressure on Black's kingside. The bishop controls the important central diagonal and prepares for potential tactics involving f7.
plans: Maintain pressure on f7
Support future d3-d4 pawn break

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black mirrors White's bishop development, placing it on the active long diagonal and targeting f2. This creates symmetrical tension and puts counter-pressure on White's kingside. The bishop supports Black's central control and prepares to meet White's aggressive intentions with equally active piece play.

The Evans Gambit begins! White sacrifices a pawn to deflect Black's active bishop and gain time for development. This bold move offers the b4 pawn to open lines and accelerate White's piece coordination. The gambit aims to create a powerful pawn center and dominate with active pieces.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates an unbalanced pawn structure, sacrificing material for development tempo and central control.
plans: Force Black's bishop to retreat and lose time
Build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black accepts the gambit pawn, winning material but allowing White to gain tempo and space. Taking on b4 is the principled response - Black gets an extra pawn but must be prepared for White's aggressive follow-up. The bishop now occupies a somewhat awkward square and will need to find a better home.
alternatives: Bd6 declines the gambit, maintaining central control

moveAnnotations.annotation: White immediately attacks the bishop, forcing it to make a decision while preparing to build a powerful pawn center. This move supports the crucial d4 advance and gives White excellent compensation for the sacrificed pawn. The c3 pawn also opens the c-file for potential rook activity later.
plans: Force Black's bishop to an inferior square
Prepare d4 to dominate the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to a5, maintaining the extra pawn while keeping the bishop somewhat active. This is the most challenging defense, as the bishop stays on the board and can potentially return to c5 later. However, the bishop is now less centralized and Black must be careful about White's mounting pressure.
alternatives: Bc5 allows d4, giving White excellent compensation

moveAnnotations.annotation: White strikes in the center, opening lines and challenging Black's e5 pawn. This powerful advance gives White excellent piece coordination and opens the position to favor active development. The d4 pawn supported by c3 creates an ideal pawn center, and White's pieces are perfectly placed to exploit the opening lines.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a classical pawn center with pawns on c3, d4, and e4, controlling key central squares and opening diagonals.
plans: Open the center to activate pieces
Exploit superior development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends the e5 pawn with a solid but passive move, avoiding immediate complications. This move supports the center but blocks the dark-squared bishop's development and leads to a cramped position. Black chooses solidity over activity, preparing to weather White's initiative.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates a solid but passive pawn structure, with d6 supporting e5 but limiting piece mobility.
alternatives: exd4 opens the center immediately but gives White excellent piece play

moveAnnotations.annotation: The key move of the Slow Variation! White brings the queen to b3, putting tremendous pressure on f7 and preparing to increase the attack. This move is more forcing than immediate castling because it creates concrete threats that Black must address. The queen eyes both f7 and the b7 pawn, creating multiple targets.
plans: Maintain pressure on f7 before castling
Prepare to intensify the attack with piece coordination

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black defends f7 with the queen, meeting White's threats head-on but placing the queen on a somewhat awkward square. This move prevents immediate tactics on f7 but the queen lacks good squares and may become a target. Black must now coordinate carefully to avoid falling behind in development.

White opens the center at the perfect moment, having maximized pressure with Qb3. This capture removes Black's central pawn and opens lines for White's pieces while Black's king remains uncastled. The timing is crucial - White has enough pressure to make this opening beneficial.
plans: Open lines for piece activity
Exploit Black's uncastled king position

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures, maintaining material balance but opening the d-file where White's pieces can become active. This recapture is practically forced, but it allows White's rook to potentially use the d-file and keeps Black's king in the center longer. The position remains dynamically balanced but White keeps the initiative.

Now White castles, having extracted maximum value from the queen's pressure on f7. With the center opened and Black's pieces somewhat uncoordinated, White's king finds safety while the rook joins the attack via the d-file. This perfectly timed castling maintains all of White's advantages while securing the king.
plans: Activate the rook on the d-file
Maintain piece pressure against Black's uncastled king

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black retreats the bishop to safety, preparing to castle and consolidate the position. The bishop finds a more secure square on b6 but remains somewhat out of play. This move shows Black's practical approach - securing the pieces and preparing to complete development rather than trying to hold onto every advantage.

White develops the bishop with tempo, targeting Black's uncastled king and controlling key light squares. This move prevents Black from castling kingside easily and maintains pressure on the eighth rank. The bishop is perfectly placed to support White's initiative and create long-term positional pressure.
plans: Prevent or complicate Black's kingside castling
Maintain pressure on light squares around Black's king

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black attacks the queen and bishop simultaneously, forcing White to make a decision while activating the knight. This aggressive move shows Black fighting for counterplay rather than passively defending. The knight on a5 attacks both b3 and c4, creating tactical opportunities and forcing White to calculate carefully.
alternatives: O-O-O attempts to castle, but White has strong pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Evans Gambit: Compromised Defence is an important variation of the Evans Gambit. Black takes the second pawn with ...dxc3 but falls behind in development. White plays Qb3 targeting f7 and Nxc3 with powerful piece play. This is extremely dangerous for Black. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black responds with e5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Evans Gambit: Compromised Defence.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Nc6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c6 eyes important squares.
Bc4 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c4 eyes important squares.
Bc5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c5 eyes important squares.
b4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Bxb4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
c3 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Ba5 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on b4 and moves to a5 where it serves the plan better.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
exd4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
dxc3 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Qb3 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on b3 eyes important squares.
Qe7 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on e7 eyes important squares. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Nxc3 captures the pawn. This exchange is thematic in the Evans Gambit: Compromised Defence — it defines the resulting pawn structure and piece activity.
Nf6. This is the typical position arising from the Evans Gambit: Compromised Defence. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

The Evans Gambit Declined is an important variation of the Evans Gambit. Black declines the gambit by retreating to b6. This is very solid and avoids the tactical complications. White gets a pleasant position with the bishop pair and open f-file. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black responds with e5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Evans Gambit Declined.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Nc6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c6 eyes important squares.
Bc4 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c4 eyes important squares.
Bc5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c5 eyes important squares.
b4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Bb6 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on c5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
a4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
a6 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Nc3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c3 eyes important squares.
Nf6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f6 eyes important squares.
d3 increases White's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
Be3 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on e3 eyes important squares.
Black castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Bxe3 captures the bishop. This exchange is thematic in the Evans Gambit Declined — it defines the resulting pawn structure and piece activity.
fxe3 completes the variation. The resulting position after this capture offers White clear targets and plans. Study this structure — you'll see it often.

The Evans Gambit: Stone-Ware Variation is an important variation of the Evans Gambit. Black retreats to d6 instead of a5, blocking the d-pawn. This leads to a quieter game but White still has the initiative with natural development and the open center. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black responds with e5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Evans Gambit: Stone-Ware Variation.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Nc6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c6 eyes important squares.
Bc4 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c4 eyes important squares.
Bc5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c5 eyes important squares.
b4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Bxb4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
c3 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Bd6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Nf6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f6 eyes important squares.
dxe5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Bxe5 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nxe5 captures the bishop. This exchange changes the balance — White reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
Nxe5 captures the knight. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Bb3 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on c4 and moves to b3 where it serves the plan better. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
d6. This is the typical position arising from the Evans Gambit: Stone-Ware Variation. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

The Evans Gambit: Lasker Defence is an important variation of the Evans Gambit. Lasker's Defence with ...d6 is one of the most reliable responses. Black maintains a solid structure and returns the pawn to complete development. The position often leads to a complex middlegame. Let's walk through the key ideas.
Black responds with e5, contesting the center. This is the move that defines the Evans Gambit: Lasker Defence.
Nf3 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on f3 eyes important squares.
Nc6 brings the knight into the game. Development with purpose — the knight on c6 eyes important squares.
Bc4 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c4 eyes important squares.
Bc5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on c5 eyes important squares.
b4 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Bxb4 captures the pawn. This exchange changes the balance — Black reconfigures the pawn structure or gains material.
c3 expands on the queenside. Gaining space here creates potential targets and restricts the opponent's counterplay.
Ba5 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on b4 and moves to a5 where it serves the plan better.
d4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d6 increases Black's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position.
White castles kingside to tuck the king away safely and activate the rook. Connecting the rooks is a priority.
Bb6 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on a5 and moves to b6 where it serves the plan better.
dxe5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
dxe5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
Qb3 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on b3 eyes important squares.
Qf6 brings the queen into the game. Development with purpose — the queen on f6 eyes important squares.
Bg5 brings the bishop into the game. Development with purpose — the bishop on g5 eyes important squares.
Qg6 activates the queen. From g6, the queen has maximum influence across multiple diagonals and files. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Bd5 increases White's influence over the center. Controlling the center is the foundation of a strong position. Notice how the pieces coordinate in this position.
Nge7. This is the typical position arising from the Evans Gambit: Lasker Defence. From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.

In the Evans Gambit, the Development Avalanche is a tactical pattern you should know. After the center opens, White's pieces pour out with tempo. Every move creates a threat, and Black struggles to complete development. The initiative is worth far more than the pawn. Let's walk through how it works.
Black plays e5, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
White plays Nf3, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
Black plays Nc6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Bc4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Bc5. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
b4 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black captures with Bxb4. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
White plays c3. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Ba5. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
d4 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black captures with exd4. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
White castles. The position looks safe, but danger lurks.
Black continues with Nge7. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
White captures with cxd4. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Black plays d5, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White captures with exd5. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Black captures with Nxd5. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Ba3 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Be6. This is where the trap begins. The next two moves are the key sequence you need to memorize.
Bb5! This is the critical move that springs the trap. The opponent is in serious trouble.
Bb6! The trap is sprung. Black has a winning position. This is the key takeaway from the Development Avalanche.

In the Evans Gambit, the Slow Evans Pressure is a tactical pattern you should know. In the Slow Evans, Qb3 creates dual threats on f7 and d5. If Black isn't precise, White wins material or gets a crushing attack. The queen and bishop battery is very hard to handle. Let's walk through how it works.
Black plays e5, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
White plays Nf3, establishing the position. The key moment is approaching.
Black plays Nc6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Bc4. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Bc5. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
b4 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black captures with Bxb4. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
White plays c3. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Ba5. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
d4 by White. The position is heading toward the critical moment.
Black plays d6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White plays Qb3. This move looks reasonable but allows the trap to unfold.
Black continues with Qd7. The trap is being set up — watch the next few moves carefully.
White captures with dxe5. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Black plays Bb6, developing normally. The opponent may not see what's coming.
White captures with exd6. This looks natural, but it walks into the trap.
Na5. This is where the trap begins. The next two moves are the key sequence you need to memorize.
Qa4! This is the critical move that springs the trap. The opponent is in serious trouble.
Nxc4! Black wins material. The trap is complete. Remember this pattern — your opponents will fall for it.

In the Evans Gambit, the d3 Counter Blockade is a dangerous line you must be aware of. Black's ...d3 blocks the center and tries to hold the extra pawn. White must maintain the initiative with active play — if the position gets closed, Black's extra pawn will matter. Let's see why.
Black plays e5. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
White plays Nf3. This is the natural continuation that leads into the warning line.
Nc6. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays Bc4. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Bc5 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with b4. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
Bxb4 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
White plays c3. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Ba5 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with d4. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
exd4 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
White castles, but the position requires careful play.
d3 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with Qb3. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
Qf6. The position is sharp and requires precise play from this point forward.
White plays e5. This is the problematic continuation you need to recognize.
Qg6 by Black. The position is becoming uncomfortable — careful defense is needed.
White continues with Re1. Watch out — a mistake here would be very costly.
Nge7. We're approaching the critical position. Pay close attention to the next moves — this is where the danger lies.
